title: Science's Quality Control Pipeline
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive vis-network flowchart showing the journey from research question through peer review, publication, replication, and scientific consensus

educational.topic: Scientific Peer Review and Consensus Building
learningObjectives: Describe the steps from initial research through peer review, publication, and replication to scientific consensus
Explain why peer review and replication are essential for reliable scientific knowledge
Evaluate the confidence level of scientific claims based on their position in the pipeline
prerequisites: Basic understanding of the scientific method
Familiarity with scientific publications and journals
Introduction to evaluating sources of information

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Interactive pipeline exploration with click-to-reveal descriptions
keyQuestions: Why does scientific confidence increase at each stage of the pipeline?
What role does replication play in distinguishing reliable findings from false positives?
How does the peer review process catch errors that the original researchers may have missed?
Why does building scientific consensus take years or decades?
commonMisconceptions: A single published study proves something is true -- publication is one step in a longer process that requires replication and consensus
Peer review guarantees a study is correct -- it reduces errors but cannot catch all problems, which is why replication matters
Scientific consensus means all scientists agree -- it means the overwhelming weight of evidence supports a conclusion
assessmentOpportunities: Have students evaluate real ecological claims and identify where in the pipeline the supporting evidence sits
Conduct a peer review role play with simplified abstracts
Ask students to create a confidence rating scale for different types of scientific sources
